refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999 availability), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation window. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to equipment immobilization or unsafe states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ customer-defined target (default 0.999). Failure modes: network outage, gateway timeout, radio interference, equipment reboot.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall implement redundant communication paths to achieve target availability.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions for availability audit.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The safety system shall enforce safe fallback behavior upon link loss.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication link with best-effort reliability (no redundancy)
DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (primary + secondary) with automatic failover and link health monitoring
DP-C: Mesh network topology with multi-hop routing and dynamic path selection
DP-D: Satellite + terrestrial hybrid link with seamless switchover logic
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (primary + secondary) with automatic failover and link health monitoring. Primary path uses licensed spectrum (e.g., LTE/5G); secondary path uses unlicensed spectrum (e.g., Wi-Fi or private radio). Link health is monitored via heartbeat; failover is triggered on primary loss within [X] ms. Both paths feed a link aggregation layer that presents a unified logical link to the application.

potential_sources: DP-B couples link availability (this FR) to network infrastructure design (separate FR), but the coupling is acceptable: the DP specifies the logical architecture (dual paths + failover), while infrastructure selection is deferred to lower-level DPs.
DP-B couples to safety fallback behavior (FR-NGHE-010.4): link loss must trigger safe state. This is a necessary coupling; the safety system must be aware of link state.
coupling_check.notes: DP-B avoids over-specification: it does not mandate specific technologies (LTE vs. 5G, Wi-Fi vs. proprietary radio), allowing flexibility in implementation. The dual-path architecture is well-established in safety-critical systems (e.g., aerospace, automotive).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Two independent communication paths can be deployed at the worksite (e.g., licensed + unlicensed spectrum available).
Assumption: Failover latency [X] ms is acceptable to the operator and safety system.
Risk: If both paths fail simultaneously (e.g., site-wide power loss, RF jamming), availability target cannot be met. Mitigation: define acceptable failure modes in safety plan.
Risk: Dual-path hardware and software complexity increases cost and maintenance burden. Mitigation: cost-benefit analysis required.
Risk: Synchronization between primary and secondary paths may introduce jitter or out-of-order delivery. Mitigation: link aggregation layer must handle reordering.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-B to specify: (1) heartbeat interval and timeout threshold for link health detection, (2) failover latency budget, (3) link aggregation algorithm (e.g., round-robin, weighted, or priority-based), (4) recovery strategy when primary link returns (immediate switch-back vs. hysteresis). These details are necessary for lower-level DP refinement.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the dual-path communication architecture, link health monitoring, failover logic, and link aggregation layer. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (to send commands and receive telemetry) and the Safety Services module (to report link state and receive safe fallback directives). It abstracts the underlying network infrastructure (primary and secondary paths) from the application layer.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (e.g., move, stop, adjust) from the tele-operator console or autonomous planner. Tasking is the input to the Teleoperation module.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Sends vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, link status) back to the tele-operator console or monitoring system. Telemetry is the output from the Teleoperation module.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface with Safety Services. Teleoperation reports link state (up/down, failover events) to Safety Services. Safety Services sends safe fallback directives (e.g., 'stop vehicle', 'enter safe mode') to Teleoperation upon link loss.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstraction of the underlying dual-path communication infrastructure. Teleoperation module manages primary and secondary network links, monitors their health, and performs failover. Network Link is internal to Teleoperation but may be realized by external infrastructure (e.g., cellular gateway, radio transceiver).

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic. The dual-path architecture, failover, and link health monitoring are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: link state reporting to Safety Services is a clean interface (SafetyAlert). Network infrastructure (primary/secondary paths) is external but managed by Teleoperation.

Safety Services could own link availability monitoring, but this would couple safety logic to network architecture. Better to keep link management in Teleoperation and have Safety Services react to link state via SafetyAlert interface. High diffusion if Safety Services owns both link monitoring and safe fallback.

Vehicle module is the controlled entity, not the controller. Link availability is a property of the communication channel to the Vehicle, not the Vehicle itself. Assigning to Vehicle would scatter teleoperation logic across multiple modules.

NGHE Solution is the top-level system. While it could own link availability as a system-level concern, it is too coarse-grained. Better to assign to Teleoperation (a subsystem) and have NGHE Solution aggregate the result.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of link availability. It encapsulates the dual-path architecture, failover logic, and link health monitoring. It provides a clean interface to the Vehicle module (Tasking/Telemetry) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert). Network infrastructure is external but managed by Teleoperation via the Network Link abstraction.
containment_risks: Risk: If network infrastructure (primary/secondary paths) is owned by a separate Network or Infrastructure module, Teleoperation must depend on that module's reliability. Mitigation: define clear SLAs for Network Link interface.

Risk: Failover logic may interact with Vehicle-level redundancy (e.g., dual onboard computers). Mitigation: clarify ownership boundary: Teleoperation owns communication failover; Vehicle owns onboard redundancy.

Risk: Link state reporting to Safety Services may create a circular dependency if Safety Services also monitors link health. Mitigation: single source of truth: Teleoperation monitors link; Safety Services reacts.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Network Link (primary and secondary paths) is either (1) owned by Teleoperation (preferred for cohesion), (2) owned by a separate Network module with clear SLA interface, or (3) owned by Vehicle (if link is vehicle-resident, e.g., onboard modem). Current assignment assumes (1) or (2).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (dual-path failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (link availability ≥ 0.999). The DP specifies a logical architecture (redundancy + failover) that is proven in safety-critical systems. The DP is not over-specified (does not mandate specific technologies) and is verifiable (link state can be monitored and availability calculated). Coupling is acceptable: DP-B couples to safety fallback, which is necessary and well-defined.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If failover logic is split between Teleoperation and Vehicle modules (e.g., Teleoperation detects link loss, Vehicle executes safe stop), coordination complexity increases. Mitigation: Teleoperation owns failover detection and initiation; Safety Services owns safe fallback execution.

Diffusion risk: If link health monitoring is duplicated across Teleoperation and Safety Services, inconsistency may arise. Mitigation: single source of truth in Teleoperation; Safety Services subscribes to SafetyAlert.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) for DP-B. The dual-path architecture, failover, and link aggregation are cohesive within Teleoperation. External dependencies (Network Link, SafetyAlert) are clean interfaces. No significant diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: link state can be logged continuously during evaluation period; availability is calculated as uptime ratio. Test scenarios: (1) primary link failure → failover to secondary, (2) secondary link failure → fallback to primary, (3) both links fail → safe state triggered. Acceptance: measured availability ≥ 0.999.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving link availability ≥ 0.999. Sub-allocations to lower-level DPs (e.g., primary path reliability, secondary path reliability, failover latency) will be defined in next decomposition level.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-B to specify failover latency budget (e.g., ≤ 100 ms) and link health detection interval (e.g., heartbeat every 10 ms).
Define acceptance criteria for 'link up' vs. 'link down' state (e.g., 3 consecutive heartbeat timeouts = link down).
Clarify interaction between Teleoperation failover and Safety Services safe fallback: who initiates safe stop upon link loss?
Confirm that Network Link interface (primary/secondary paths) is owned by Teleoperation or a separate module with clear SLA.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (link loss detection, redundant paths, logging, safe fallback). DP-B is sound and well-coupled to the refined FR. Teleoperation is the correct IM owner with high containment (0.95). However, the DP requires further refinement at the next level to specify failover latency, link health detection, and path selection algorithms. Proceeding to decompose sub-FRs is necessary to allocate these details and verify feasibility.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds (e.g., 100 ms).
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall implement redundant communication paths (primary + secondary) with automatic failover.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions (up/down, failover events) for availability audit.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The safety system shall enforce safe fallback behavior (e.g., stop vehicle) upon link loss detection.

assumptions: Two independent communication paths (primary + secondary) can be deployed at the worksite.
Failover latency of [X] ms is acceptable to the operator and safety system.
Link state can be monitored and logged continuously during evaluation period.
Network infrastructure (primary/secondary paths) is available and managed by Teleoperation or a separate Network module.
Safety Services is responsible for executing safe fallback upon link loss; Teleoperation is responsible for detecting and reporting link loss.
open_risks: Simultaneous failure of both communication paths (e.g., site-wide power loss, RF jamming) cannot be mitigated by DP-B alone. Mitigation: define acceptable failure modes in safety plan and worksite operational policies.
Failover latency may exceed acceptable threshold if network infrastructure is congested or degraded. Mitigation: SLA for Network Link interface; monitoring and alerting.
Synchronization and reordering issues in link aggregation layer may cause command/telemetry corruption. Mitigation: link aggregation algorithm must handle out-of-order delivery; end-to-end checksums.
Cost and complexity of dual-path architecture may exceed project budget. Mitigation: cost-benefit analysis; consider phased deployment (single path initially, dual path in future).
evidence_needed: Worksite network infrastructure assessment: availability of primary and secondary communication paths.
Failover latency measurement: time from primary link loss to secondary link activation.
Link availability measurement during evaluation period: continuous logging of link state transitions.
Safety plan and operational policies: acceptable failure modes, safe fallback procedures, operator training.
Cost-benefit analysis: dual-path architecture cost vs. availability improvement.
Proof-of-concept or prototype: demonstrate failover logic and link aggregation in representative environment.
